Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs LouvrrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
July 16,2007
Bill Gearheart
Director, Board of Directors
Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District
P.O. Box 244
Bethel Island, California 9451 1 -0244
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-07-086
Dear Mr. Gearheart:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should
be construed to evaluate any conduct thatmay have already taken pla,ce, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.2 The Fair political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it
renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.) Because you seek general
guidance rather than advice on a specific governmental decision, we provide you with
informal assistance.3 Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions
of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application, if arty, of other bodies of law such as
cofilmon law conflicts of interest, or Government code section 1090.
Please be advised that this letter is limited to the facts that you have provided to
us. In this letter we consider only your interest in your real property, andthe potential
conflicts of interests associated with your interest in that property. You have not
disclosed any other economic interests. For example, if you have business interests that
are, or have been, affected by a decision in a manner that has a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, you may not participate in the decision because you already
have a conflict of interest in that decision, based on a different subdivision of Section
87103. In addition, this advice is not intended to impact any issue which may be the
subject of a pending enforcement matter.
I Government Code sections 8 I 000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections
I 8 1 09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
' The Co--ission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA),
copy enclosed.)
3 Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).)
g9


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 2
QUESTTON
Are you prohibited from participating in a governmental decision by the Bethel
Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created
water system?
CONCLUSION
You have a conflict of interest andmay not participate in a govefllmental decision
involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water
entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies.
FACTS
You are the Director and avoting member of the Board of Directors for the
Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District (BIMID). Bethel Island has
approximately 1,600 parcels of land. The uses of the land include agricultural,
commercial, and residential. There is no municipal or corporate water service on the
island. Most of the island is served by small private water companies, wells controlled
by mobile home park owners, or private wells.
A large developer has a tentative agreement with Diablo Water/Contra Costa
Water for service to a new development on the island that would include 490 single
family homes and 100 condominiums. The developer is now considering construction of
a high quality water treatment and distribution system that would include separate
irrigation water and fire hydrants for its project. The developer has communicated with a
number of existing small mutual water companies currently providing water to nearby
properties to see ifthey wish to become part of the new water system.
The initial discussion with the small municipal water companies, along with the
project when built out, would serve approximately 80 percent of the populated portion of
the island. The remaining2} percent of the populated portion of the island would not
have access to the new system. Those properties, apart from eighteen homes that receive
water from Diablo Water/Contra Costa Water, would continue to use untreated water
with high quantities of dissolved solids. You report that this could result in a negative
impact to the property values in the unserved areainrelation to the properties that receive
new services.
The developer has indicated a willingness to expand its water system to the
remaining 20 percent of the island, or at least to make it possible for future expansion.
BIMID believes that the developer is ready to create a water system for 80 percent of the
island regardless of BIMID involvement, but if BIMID agrees to accept operational
control of the new water system, the developer would put in place equipment that would
allow the entire island to be connected to the new water system.


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 3
_ 
In summary, as we understand it, the decision in question,fr&ycome before
BIMID only after the developer has built or committed to uuito ;""* wateisystem
serving 80 percent of the island's residents, and the decision would be to determine if
BIMID will operate the system. The effect of the decision to have BIMID assume
control of the water system would be the expansion of the system to serve the remaining
20o/o of the island.
- 
You own your own home, which is among the 2}percent of homes that would not
be receiving the new water system if BIMID doeinot u""!pt operational control. you
would like to know whether you have a conflict of interestin participating in a decision
to have BIMID assume control of the water system if the developr, *.r"io make that a
condition to expanding the new water system to the entire island.
ANALYSIs
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, frie from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest ofpersons who have supported them. (section arooiOl.l
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from making, panicipating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence ufor".rrm"ntut
decision in which the officiar has a financial interest.
A public official has a 'ofinancial interest" iL agovemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable"'that the govemmental decision
will have amateial financial effect on one ormore of the public official,s economic
interests' (section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) tn order to determine whether a public
lfficialhas a disquali$ring conflict of interesi i" u gi""" lovernmental decision, the
commission has adopted an eight-step analyticalfrLew"ork. (Regulatio" isTooOXr>
(8)') The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
gfficia] makes a govemmental decision that has a reasonably ror.r""uti" *urlriur
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section g7103.)
Step One: Are You a public Official?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to public officials. (Section
87100') Public officials include members orcorrrmiuees,'boards or commissions with
decision making authority. (Regulation 1s701(a)(1).) A; ,;;;;#rrr" 
riivrro Board
of Directors, you are a public official under ttre'Act. '


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 4
Step Two: will You Be Making, participating in Making, or using your
Official Position to Influence a Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contiactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.L) As a boardmember of
BIMID you will be making a govemmental decision if you vote on whether BIMID
should assume operational control of the water system.
In addition, a public official "participates in making" a govemmental decision when
he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Therefore, you will be participating in making
a govemmental decision if you advise, recornmend or negotiate a course oiaction for
BIMID with regard to taking responsibility for the water system.a
Furthermore, a public official is using her or her official position to influence a
govemmental decision, before his or her agency, if he or she contacts, or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of
BIMID regarding the decisions. (Regulation 18702.3(a)) Therefore, you would be
influencing a govemmental decision if you contact, appear before, or otherwise attempt
to influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of BIMID regarding the water
system decision.
Step Three: What Are Your Economic fnterests?
A public official has a "financial interest" in agovemmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate familS or on any one of five enumerated bconomic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87r03(a); Regulation
1 8703. 1 (a)), or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section s7 1 03 (d);
Regulation 1 8703. 1 (b));
a Exception: M+ing or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official,s wholly
owned real property or business enng. (See Regulation 18702.4)


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 5
o 
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
Indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section s7103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
o 
An economic interest in any source of income, includingpromised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within tz mo'nihs prior to the
decision (S ection g7 I 03 (c) ; Regulation I S7 03 .3) ;
o 
An economic interest in any:gurce of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $390 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
871 03(e); Regulation 187 B.g;
o 
I public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects,, rule.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation 1 g703.5.)
Assuming you own at reast a $2,000 interest in your home, you have a
potentially disqualifying economic interest in your real property.
- 
Everypublic official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family. (section 87|I3;Regulation iszoi.i)- However,
Regulation 18705.5 provides that when determining whether a decision would have a
material financial effect on an official's economic iirterest in his or rr"r p"rrorut
finances, a financial effect on the value of real property owned by the official will not be
considered. Because the only other economic inteiest-you have indicated to us is your
interest in real property on the island, we will consider potential financial effects on that
property only, and will not further discuss potential edcts on your personal finances or
other potential economic interests.
Step Four: Is Your Economic Interest Directly or fndirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?
Regulation 18704.2(6) states that apublic official has an economic interest that is
directly involved in a governmental decision if "the decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets , watet, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, ura th" ,.ut
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improvedservices.,, As
we understand the facts, should BIMID decide to assume control of the new water
system, there would be improvements to water services to your property. This is because
BIMID's agreement to assume control over the new system *outd 6" u pr.-.ondition to
an expansion of the system to provide service to the 20 percentof the island not covered
in the original build out and this 20 percent would include your property. Therefore, your
economic interest in your real property would be directly invotvea in this gorrl;*rrtut
decision.


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 6
Step tr'ive: Applicable Materiality Standard.
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is "material." (Section
87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect
of a governmental decision on real property thatis directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be mateiaT. Under the regulation, this presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have any financial effect, even one penny's worth, on the real property.
Step Six: Is it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financial Effect of the
Governmental Decision Upon Your Economic Interests WilI Be Material?
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govefirmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) Your account of the facts indicates that if BIMID decides
not to assume control of the water system, properties outside the service area would
continue to use untreated water and could be adversely affected by that decision.
Accordingly, there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your real property.
Step Seven: The Public Generally Exception.
The "public generally exception" allows officials who may otherwise have a
disqualifuing interest to nonetheless participate in a governmental decision if their
interest is affected in the same manner as the "public generally." Regulation lB7O7
provides:
"Notwithstanding a determination that the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest
is material, a public official does not have a disqualifiiing conflict of
interest in the governmental decision if the official canestablish that the
govefirmental decision will affect the public official's economic interest in
a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the
decision will affect the public generally as set forth in 2 cal. code Regs.
Sections 187 07 .l-187 07 .9 ]'
Regulation 18707.1provides the rule to determine if the material financial effect
of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally. Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 7
on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if it will affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantialiy the same
manner" as it will affect the public official's economic interest.
For real property economic interests, the significant segment is defined under
Regulation 18707.r (b)(l)(B) as:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
As you have explained, approximately 20 percerftof the properties on the island
would be affected by BIMID's decision of whether or not to assume control of the new
water system, insofar as that decision would determine their access to the new water
system. Thus, it appears that a significant segment of the property owners or
homeowners on the island would be affected by this decision.
If the "significant segment" prong of this two-part test is met, it is next necessary
to determine if the significant segment is affected in substantially the same manner as the
public official's economic interest. The financial effect need noi be identical for the
official's economic interest to be considered affected in "substantially the same manner.,,
(Regulation 187 07 .t (bX2).)
The Berger Advice Letter, No. 4-05-054, andthe Myers Advice Letter,
No. 4-06-107, advised that the financial effect on real property within a..significant
segment" could varyby a specified financial range equal to plus or minus two percent of
the value of the public official's property and still be considered to be affected in
"substantially the same manner" as the official's property. For example, if an official,s
property is valued at $500,000, and the financial effect of the decision is a three-percent
increase ($15,000) on the official's property, increases between $5,000 and $25,000
($10,000 (2%) plus or minus)) on properties within the significant segment would be
considered substantially the same. As can be seen, these effects are measured in terms of
values and not percentages.5
Because all of the properties in the significant segment of the population will be
receiving the same new water services it is likely these properties wili either increase or
5 For example, assume that the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a govemmental decision
results in a five-percent increase inproperty values for the city as a whole. The finan-cial effect on a
$1,000,000 property would be twice as much as the financial Lff".t on a $500,000 prop"rty, ura would not
be considered substantially the same, even though the percentage change is the same. Conversely, a
financial effect of six-pe-rcent on a $500,000 property would be substantiatty the same as a three-percent
increase on a $1,000,000 properfy, even though the percent increase is not tie same. (See.B erger, Id.)


File No. I-07-086
Page No. 8
decrease in value by a uniform percentage. However, because you have not provided us
with the relative property values of the properties affected, we are unable to determine
whether or not the "substantially same manner" test has been met. If you need further
assistance please write back and include those facts. Should you determine that the
homeowners in your significant segment are affected in substantially the same manner
then the public generally exception will apply, and you would not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision.
Step Eight: LegaIIy Required Participation
The final step of the conflicts analysis provides an exception for legally required
participation. At this point, we have no indication that this exception would bL invoked
to enable BIMID to make this decision, and we therefore end our analysis at this point.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
SincerelS
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKBjel

is material, a public official does not have a disqualifiiing conflict of

a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the
decision will affect the public generally as set forth in 2 cal. code Regs.
Sections 187 07 .l-187 07 .9 ]'
Regulation 18707.1provides the rule to determine if the material financial effect
of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from its effect on the public generally. Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision
on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public

For real property economic interests, the significant segment is defined under

jurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
As you have explained, approximately 20 percerftof the properties on the island
would be affected by BIMID's decision of whether or not to assume control of the new
water system, insofar as that decision would determine their access to the new water
system. Thus, it appears that a significant segment of the property owners or
homeowners on the island would be affected by this decision.
If the "significant segment" prong of this two-part test is met, it is next necessary
to determine if the significant segment is affected in substantially the same manner as the
public official's economic interest. The financial effect need noi be identical for the
official's economic interest to be considered affected in "substantially the same manner.,,
(Regulation 187 07 .t (bX2).)
The Berger Advice Letter, No. 4-05-054, andthe Myers Advice Letter,
No. 4-06-107, advised that the financial effect on real property within a..significant
segment" could varyby a specified financial range equal to plus or minus two percent of
the value of the public official's property and still be considered to be affected in
"substantially the same manner" as the official's property. For example, if an official,s
property is valued at $500,000, and the financial effect of the decision is a three-percent
increase ($15,000) on the official's property, increases between $5,000 and $25,000
($10,000 (2%) plus or minus)) on properties within the significant segment would be
considered substantially the same. As can be seen, these effects are measured in terms of
values and not percentages.5
Because all of the properties in the significant segment of the population will be
receiving the same new water services it is likely these properties wili either increase or
5 For example, assume that the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a govemmental decision
results in a five-percent increase inproperty values for the city as a whole. The finan-cial effect on a
$1,000,000 property would be twice as much as the financial Lff".t on a $500,000 prop"rty, ura would not
be considered substantially the same, even though the percentage change is the same. Conversely, a
financial effect of six-pe-rcent on a $500,000 property would be substantiatty the same as a three-percent
increase on a $1,000,000 properfy, even though the percent increase is not tie same. (See.B erger, Id.)
decrease in value by a uniform percentage. However, because you have not provided us
with the relative property values of the properties affected, we are unable to determine
whether or not the "substantially same manner" test has been met. If you need further
assistance please write back and include those facts. Should you determine that the
homeowners in your significant segment are affected in substantially the same manner
then the public generally exception will apply, and you would not be prohibited from
participating in the governmental decision.
Step Eight: LegaIIy Required Participation
The final step of the conflicts analysis provides an exception for legally required
participation. At this point, we have no indication that this exception would bL invoked
to enable BIMID to make this decision, and we therefore end our analysis at this point.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are you prohibited from participating in a governmental decision by the Bethel
Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created
water system?

CONCLUSION: You have a conflict of interest andmay not participate in a govefllmental decision
involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water
entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies.
embedding.first_500_words: Fern Polrrrcel PRACTTcEs LouvrrssroN 428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886 July 16,2007 Bill Gearheart Director, Board of Directors Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District P.O. Box 244 Bethel Island, California 9451 1 -0244 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our FiIe No.I-07-086 Dear Mr. Gearheart: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct thatmay have already taken pla,ce, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.2 The Fair political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.) Because you seek general guidance rather than advice on a specific governmental decision, we provide you with informal assistance.3 Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We offer no opinion on the application, if arty, of other bodies of law such as cofilmon law conflicts of interest, or Government code section 1090. Please be advised that this letter is limited to the facts that you have provided to us. In this letter we consider only your interest in your real property, andthe potential conflicts of interests associated with your interest in that property. You have not disclosed any other economic interests. For example, if you have business interests that are, or have been, affected by a decision in a manner that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, you may not participate in the decision because you already have a conflict of interest in that decision, based on a different subdivision of Section 87103. In addition, this advice is not intended to impact any issue which may be the subject of a pending enforcement matter. I Government Code sections 8 I 000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections I 8 1 09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations. ' The Co--ission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8XA), copy enclosed.) 3 Informal assistance does not offer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3).) g9 File No. I-07-086 Page No. 2 QUESTTON Are you prohibited from participating in a governmental decision by the Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District to assume operational control of a newly created water system? CONCLUSION You have a conflict of interest andmay not participate in a govefllmental decision involving the Bethel Island Municipal District assuming operational control of a water entity for the District, unless the public generally exception applies. FACTS You are the Director and avoting member of the Board of Directors for the Bethel Island Municipal Improvement District (BIMID). Bethel Island has approximately 1,600 parcels of land. The uses of the land include agricultural, commercial, and residential. There is no municipal or corporate water service on the island. Most of the island is
